source.title: Auriga Health - Runway & Stability Sprint Handoff
next_steps: Maya to send Dedicated pilot pricing, commit tiers and SLA (by 2027-02-18)
Ethan to run 1-week latency benchmark with Auriga's v1 prompt set (target start 2027-02-22)
Priya to share runbook template and access instructions for staging VPC (by 2027-02-17)
Customer to confirm pilot team and success criteria (by 2027-02-25)
Security to receive SOC2 + network diagram in shared drive (Maya to attach)
action_items: Maya Chen: Draft Dedicated pilot SOW and pricing (due 2027-02-18)
Ethan Park: Spin up benchmark harness + run baseline with 95th p99 latency (due 2027-02-24)
Priya Nair: Provision access to Redwood Console trial for Auriga staging (due 2027-02-16)
James O'Neal: Provide IP allowlist and preferred region for VPC peering (due 2027-02-19)
Lina Gomez: Send list of compliance questions for SOC2/ISO and KMS integration (due 2027-02-20)
source.summary: Quarterly health + runway sync focused on right-sizing a Dedicated pilot to stabilize latency and cost. Customer concerned about recent token spend spikes and p99 tail latency; security team requested hard guarantees and KMS/HSM detail. Agreed next steps: pricing SOW, latency benchmark, VPC staging access, and pilot kickoff planning.
source.transcript:
Meeting header: 2027-02-14 16:00 PST / Duration: ~52m / Attendees: Maya, Ethan, Priya (Redwood); Carlos, Sofia, James, Lina (Auriga)

Summary: quick recap -> usage jump in January, looking to stabilize for product launch, want Dedicated pilot + runbook, security vetting.

[00:00] Maya (AE): hey everyone, thanks for joining um this is our quarterly runway and stability handoff, we'll keep to an hour. quick intros?
[00:18] Carlos (Auriga): yeah Carlos here, head of infra - we saw a spike last month when feature flag went wide and need to make sure the latency tail doesn't kill user flows.
[00:29] Sofia (Auriga): Sofia, product - want predictable cost for the launch window and clear rollback policies.
[00:39] James (Auriga): James, cloud ops - I'll own the VPC peering and infra side.
[00:44] Lina (Auriga): Lina, security - we'll be looking at KMS, audit logs, and network diagrams.
[00:49] Ethan (SE): hi all, Ethan here - I'll go through the perf numbers and propose batching/kv cache configs. apologies if I mis-say any acronyms.
[00:58] Priya (CSM): and I'll track the pilot dates and runbook handoff.

[01:05] Maya: quick status - January spikes: ~3x token consumption on the routing microservice, cost up ~45% month-over-month. important context: most traffic is midsize prompts around 1k tokens, but we saw bursts with multi-turns.
[01:32] Carlos: yep the bursts were from our triage assistant, the cache warmed but not enough; we saw p99 jump from 360ms to 1.2s.
[01:44] Ethan: that's consistent with KV cache thrash; with Redwood runtime we can keep prefix cache pinned per user shard, reduce K-V cash misses. also continuous batching will help amortize small requests.
[02:01] Sofia: we care about two things: 95th/p99 latency and monthly unit economics. what does Dedicated give us vs hosted?
[02:12] Maya: Dedicated means reserved GPU pools so you get predictable throughput and lower p99s because no noisy neighbors, plus SLA. Hosted is faster to get started but can have more variance. cost math depends on commit and utilization.
[02:28] Lina: security ask: can you describe KMS integration, H-S-M support, and where keys live for Private vs Dedicated?
[02:38] Ethan: brief: Dedicated can integrate with customer KMS via our private control plane (we support cloud KMS and H-S-Ms for on-prem), keys never leave customer-managed HSM, Redwood gets encrypted blobs. note: our docs list supported providers but we'll attach the network diagram.
[02:59] James: for VPC peering - what's the egress model? do we need fixed IPs?
[03:06] Maya: yes, we can provide IP allowlists and peering; for Dedicated you can have private endpoints. we'll send the exact ranges and peering steps.

[03:25] Ethan: jumping to perf: baseline we ran in staging last week shows median token latency ~120ms on a 8xA100 config but p99 sits at ~950ms under a synthetic burst. with smarter batching + prefix cache we drop p99 to ~260ms in our estimate.
[03:48] Carlos: that would be ideal; p99 under 300ms is our SLA target. are those numbers with quantized weights?
[03:57] Ethan: good call - the tests used fp16 but we can run int8 quant profiles; quantization knocks down GPU memory and can improve throughput but sometimes increases per-token latency slightly depending on kernels. we'll test both.
[04:17] Sofia: pragmatic question: what's the commitment look like — pilot vs reserved? we don't want to overcommit.
[04:24] Maya: pilot options: 4-week Dedicated pilot with a light commit tier to reserve capacity but with a credit back if we miss SLAs; or hosted pilot with burst capacity. pilot is to validate latency, regression, cost.
[04:40] Lina: and auditability - we need access to request logs, model versioning, and eval traces for compliance. is that in Redwood Console?
[04:50] Priya: yes, Redwood Console exposes token-level breakdown, per-model traces, and rollout history. we can give staging access.

[05:03] [05:15] Ethan: on rollouts — we support canary and automatic fallback to cheaper model variant if throughput is constrained. key thing is tying regressions to prompt sets; we'll set up quality checks so you don't roll forward bad outputs.
[05:30] Carlos: what's the cost delta between an optimized workload with batching/cache vs naive per-request?
[05:36] Ethan: rough ballpark — with continuous batching and KV cache you can see 35-60% token cost reduction depending on request distribution; caching helps especially with repeated prefixes.
[05:48] Sofia: we have a lot of repeated system prompts across sessions, that seems promising.

[06:00] Maya: let's talk timeline: you want a pilot before launch in mid-March?
[06:06] Carlos: yes ideally a 4-week pilot starting end of Feb so we can iterate.
[06:11] Maya: ok we'll aim to start benchmarks week of Feb 22, pilot kickoff Mar 1.

[06:22] [06:45] James: operations — during pilot, will Redwood provide on-call and runbook collaboration?
[06:29] Priya: yes we'll share an oncall matrix, and Ethan/SEs will be on weekly syncs and pager duty for critical perf regressions during ramp.
[06:40] Carlos: do you have playbooks for autoscale and burst scenarios?
[06:45] Ethan: we do, autoscale policies support thresholds, cool-down windows, and burst buffers; we can demo the autoscale simulation in the pilot.

[07:00] Lina: security detail: encryption in transit and at rest — do you keep any request logs in plaintext? retention windows?
[07:09] Maya: by default we store minimal logs for billing, but for Dedicated we support configurable retention and can redact PII; all data at rest is encrypted with customer-managed KMS if requested. we'll attach SOC2 docs.
[07:28] Lina: also need proof of pen test and ISO if available.
[07:33] Maya: we'll attach SOC2 + penetration summary; ISO cert is in progress, we'll note the status.

[07:50] [08:05] Sofia: on model versioning — how do rollbacks work if a new model introduces hallucinations in production?
[07:57] Ethan: Console keeps versioned model manifests, you can pin versions per route, and Automatic fallback will route to a stable variant if eval thresholds fail. rollbacks are immediate with our routing layer.
[08:10] Carlos: how to integrate with our A/B telemetry? we want to attribute regressions to model changes
[08:16] Ethan: we can add trace ids and push model metadata into your telemetry; we'll collaborate on instrumentation.

[08:35] Maya: cost discussion: we'll model two scenarios — low commit (hosted + burst) vs 3-month reserved Dedicated. pricing sheet will include per-token delta and amortized hardware cost.
[08:48] Sofia: please include run-rate scenarios at 10k, 50k, 200k daily tokens.
[08:55] Maya: noted.

[09:05] [09:20] Priya: we'll provision staging access to Redwood Console today for Aurora — sorry Auriga — (brief laugh) staging, so you can see traces and the consoles token breakdown.
[09:33] James: small ask — do you support private peering in our eu-west-1 region?
[09:38] Maya: yes we have regions in NA and EU; for Dedicated we'll map to the eu-west region. we'll confirm availability.

[09:50] Ethan: technical deep dive highlight — common mitigations: 1) reduce seq length for system messages via truncation, 2) prefix caching, 3) mixed precision and kernel selection, 4) batching windows tuned to P95 targets. each has tradeoffs.
[10:15] Carlos: curious about kernel selection — do you pick automatically?
[10:20] Ethan: runtime will select kernels based on model arch and sequence distribution; we can force configurations in Dedicated for determinism.

[10:35] [11:00] Sofia: regarding QA — we want gating so that model rollouts run through our evals before hitting prod. how soon can we integrate our prompt sets?
[10:48] Ethan: you can upload prompt sets to Console; we'll hook them into rollout gating and quality monitoring. pilot will run those evals nightly.

[11:05] Lina: re compliance: we need a signed DPA and data residency commitments.
[11:11] Maya: standard DPA supports EU data residency; for stronger requirements we'll offer Private deployment options with customer-controlled storage. we'll include DPA template.

[11:30] Carlos: what about throttling behaviour — if we exceed capacity will requests queue or fail fast?
[11:37] Ethan: can be configured; for low-latency flows we recommend graceful degradation to a simpler model variant or immediate 429 with retry-after, rather than long queues.

[11:55] [12:20] Sofia: ok on success criteria — customer definition: p99 < 300ms for main route during peak, cost within 20% of forecasted run-rate, zero privacy incidents, and onboarding complete for ops.
[12:36] Maya: that's clear. we'll bake that into pilot SOW.

[12:50] Priya: scheduling note — we'll do weekly syncs during pilot (30 minutes), and a mid-pilot review at week 2.

[13:05] Maya: quick demo snippet — Console view, token breakdown, per-route latency heatmap (screen shared).
[13:20] [13:40] (screen demo, occasional audio), Ethan: here you see model rollout timeline and the per-prompt failure rate. the traces tie back to request ids.

[14:02] Carlos: looks good. any limitations for long-context chat history?
[14:09] Ethan: large contexts increase memory and can change kernel paths; we recommend KV cache for multi-turn state and offload embeddings where possible.

[14:25] Sofia: one more — how do you handle billing disputes or anomalous surges?
[14:31] Maya: we have a billing dispute process and credits for true infra incidents; surges caused by customer traffic are billable but we'll provide credits for platform-level incidents. documented in the SLA.

[14:50] [15:10] James: Ops question — can I run a dry-run of peering in our staging before pilot?
[14:56] Priya: yes, we'll provision a staging VPC and share the peering steps.

[15:20] Maya: ok wrapping up — to confirm next steps: Maya will share pricing SOW; Ethan runs the benchmark; Priya sets up Console access; James shares IPs; Lina sends compliance list. pilot kick-off targeted Mar 1.
[15:38] Carlos: sounds good. thanks everyone.

[15:45] (informal chat)
[16:00] End call.

Notes: small transcription glitches (Auriga sometimes transcribed as Aurora in the console). references to K-V cash, Samul instead of SAML in a few places. Attachments: Pricing draft (to be sent), SOC2 summary, network diagram link (placeholder).

source.title: 2026-04-09-steady-state-throttling-prefetch-heartbeat-maxnumseqs-tune
next_steps: Redwood to share scheduler knob baseline and sample perf script
Customer to run 30m steady-state load and share token histograms
Follow-up call to review results and prefetch tuning week of 2026-04-20
action_items: Maya Chen: send scheduler-knobs-playbook and small benchmark script by 2026-04-13
Ethan Park: produce kv-cache hit-rate checklist and suggested prewarm heartbeat values by 2026-04-14
Devon Li: run 30 minute steady state with 95th percentile latency target and attach traces by 2026-04-17
source.summary: Deep dive focused on steady-state throughput tuning for on-prem VPC deployment: continuous batching behavior, scheduler parameters (max_num_seqs, token caps), prefetch/heartbeat for KV cache, and quantization fallback tradeoffs. Customer constrained by strict SLOs (95th < 250ms) and data residency; interested in Redwood private+dedicated hybrid. Action plan: exchange knobs, run steady-state test, and reconvene.
source.transcript:
Meeting header:
Date: 2026-04-09
Start time: 15:00 PDT
Duration: 58 minutes
Attendees: Maya Chen, Ethan Park (Redwood) — Devon Li, Priya Kapoor, Carlos Mendes, Lena Ortiz (Auriga)

Summary (auto): Deep technical tuning session. Discussed continuous batching, scheduler knobs (max_num_seqs, admission thresholds), token ceilings/fairness, KV cache prefetch/heartbeat, and quantization fallbacks for on-prem deployment.

--- Transcript body (automated) ---
[00:00] Maya Chen: ok everyone thanks for joinin, uh we'll jump right in. I'm Maya, AE on this account, with Ethan from our infra team. Devon, wanna quickly restate goals?
[00:15] Devon Li: yeah thanks, um main ask is we need stable throughput for our clinical summarization pipeline. 95th P for us should be under 250 ms end-to-end for single-turn summarization, and we run bursts during daytime. We're on a VPC private deployment in our colo, so we care about predictable GPU usage and KV hit rates.
[00:35] Ethan Park: got it. And current infra? Priya, can you outline the stack real quick — model sizes, quantization, that sort of stuff.
[00:45] Priya Kapoor: sure, so we're serving an Llama-2-13B variant, int8 quant, running on A100-ish nodes, single tenant for the inference pods. We currently have a small prefix cache enabled, but observed KV hit rates around 25-30% which feels low. Also sometimes we hit the token ceiling we set, which causes 429s or queuing.
[01:10] Carlos Mendes: and to add, network egress is limited in the colo so cross-region fallback is problematic. We want smart on-node prefetch and warmer cold-start behavior.
[01:22] Ethan Park: okay, that helps. We'll dig into scheduler knobs — especially max_num_seqs and admission windows. Quick primer: continuous batching groups unfinished sequences on GPU to get throughput with minimal latency impact. max_num_seqs controls how many concurrent sequences we allow per slot before we cap; token limits are the per-request guard to avoid floods.
[01:45] Lena Ortiz: small question — can you define admission window? I hear it a lot but wanna be sure we're aligned.
[01:50] Ethan Park: yeah. admission window is the time slice the scheduler waits to accumulate requests before dispatch; shorter window = lower latency but less packing efficiency. It's tunable and often adaptive. On-prem network jitter means sometimes the window needs to be slightly longer to let stragglers in, but too long hurts tail.
[02:10] Devon Li: are there safe defaults for on-prem vs hosted? We don't want to guess.
[02:15] Maya Chen: we have baseline presets — 'hosted-low-latency', 'dedicated-steady', and 'onprem-stable' — we'll share the exact knob set after. For onprem-stable we bias toward slightly larger admission windows and conservative max_num_seqs to avoid noisy neighbor impact.
[02:32] Ethan Park: important nuance: max_num_seqs interacts with token-floor/ceil. If you increase max_num_seqs to boost throughput, you also may need higher per-slot token caps or else you'll see token-guard rejections.
[02:46] Priya Kapoor: we saw that — when we upped batch size the 95th spiked due to token-guard requeues. Is that expected?
[02:53] Ethan Park: yep, what's likely happening is sequences get packed but hit the token budget mid-run and scheduler has to stall or evict. The choices are to raise token budgets (risking head-of-line blocking) or implement soft token ceilings plus fairness windows so long requests don't starve.
[03:12] Devon Li: hmm, soft ceilings meaning degrade throughput slowly?
[03:15] Ethan Park: basically. Soft ceilings use a probabalistic admission after the soft threshold — some requests may be allowed with lower priority or routed to a fallback quantized variant.
[03:25] Maya Chen: which brings up quantization fallback — do you have deterministic quality constraints? i.e. how much degradation is acceptable if we route to int4 or a smaller model for burst capacity?
[03:37] Lena Ortiz: clinically, we need fidelity for certain categories, but for non-critical summarization it's okay to have slightly lower fidelity. We can tag requests by criticality.
[03:45] Carlos Mendes: our main worry is variance — bursts that create heavy tail latency. If fallback reduces latency variability but increases mean error a bit, that's an acceptable trade for some flows.
[03:56] Ethan Park: perfect use case for per-route fallback policies. Redwood runtime supports policy-based routing: route to primary model, if token budget or max latency threshold would be exceeded, fallback to quantized or smaller model.
[04:08] Devon Li: ok, let's drill into KV cache. Our hit rate of 25% comes from short-lived conversation prefixes? We saw a lot of slightly different prompts.
[04:18] Priya Kapoor: yes different patient names and small variations reduce prefix reuse. Also our prefix window is only 64 tokens.
[04:25] Ethan Park: two levers: smarter prefix canonicalization (normalize patient names/PHI) and prefetch heartbeats. Prefetch heartbeats periodically refresh likely prefixes into local KV to avoid cold misses.
[04:36] Maya Chen: for onprem, heartbeat frequency needs to account for node churn and network cost — too frequent increases jitter. Typical starting point is heartbeat every 30s with a TTL of 5 minutes for prefetch candidates.
[04:49] Carlos Mendes: 30s? that seems aggressive for our network. Could we do 60s?
[04:52] Ethan Park: yeah 60s is fine if your working set is small; the tradeoff is initial miss latency. Also look at eviction policy — LRU with weighted recency for longer medical prefixes can help.
[05:05] Devon Li: any tooling to measure prefetch efficacy easily?
[05:08] Ethan Park: we'll share a small script that emits KV hit-rate histograms and cold-start tail traces, you can run it during a 30 minute steady-state, and that'll tell us if heartbeat freq is too low.
[05:19] Maya Chen: action item there — Ethan will send the script.
[05:22] Devon Li: great. Now on continuous batching: we noticed throughput sometimes drops mid-run — scheduler logs show adaptive window contraction. Is that normal?
[05:31] Ethan Park: yes we have adaptive heuristics that contract windows when latency targets are at risk. But onprem network variance can cause oscillation: scheduler shortens window because a straggler didn't arrive, then later more requests pile up and it lengthens, causing throughput churn.
[05:45] Priya Kapoor: can we pin the heuristic? we prefer stability over absolute peak throughput.
[05:49] Ethan Park: you can switch to a deterministic packing with fixed windows, or tune the hysteresis parameters — increase damping so the window doesn't oscillate quickly. The knobs are hysteresis_ms and backoff_multiplier.
[06:00] Lena Ortiz: if we pin windows, do we risk higher latency on light loads?
[06:04] Ethan Park: yes. On light load fixed windows increase median latency. So it's a tradeoff. For Auriga we can stage a middle ground: fixed windows during peak hours, adaptive at night.
[06:15] Devon Li: nice. We also have a policy-level requirement: per-customer token fairness — can Redwood enforce soft quotas?
[06:22] Maya Chen: yes, we support per-customer soft token quotas and weighted fair queueing. You can set token budgets per API key and a grace window for bursting. Enforcement is in the admission path.
[06:33] Carlos Mendes: what happens when multiple heavy requests from single customer flood a node? Does it impact other tenants?
[06:39] Ethan Park: with per-customer shards and soft quotas you shouldn't see neighbor impact; the scheduler can isolate by slot weight. In your private single-tenant setup it's more about preventing single reqs from blocking the GPU; that's where max_num_seqs and token-ceil guards help.
[06:52] Priya Kapoor: can you explain max_num_seqs in practice? we had it set to 8 and saw long tail, when set to 4 we lost throughput but improved p95.
[07:02] Ethan Park: max_num_seqs is the cap on concurrent sequences per execution slot. Lowering it reduces contention for KV and memory, so p95 improves but throughput (tokens/s) drops because you're underutilizing GPU. If your workload has many long sequences, you want lower max_num_seqs. If many short sequences, higher is better.
[07:20] Devon Li: we have mixed lengths — some short patient notes, some long discharge summaries. Any heuristic?
[07:25] Ethan Park: measure mean seq length and 90th percentile token length; if 90th is > 512 tokens consider max_num_seqs=2-4. For majority <128 tokens you can push to 8-12. We'll run sample profiling.
[07:38] Maya Chen: also consider sequence packing policies — interleaved vs contiguous packing. Interleaved reduces KV thrash for mixed lengths.
[07:46] Priya Kapoor: can we do per-route max_num_seqs? e.g., critical short requests get higher concurrency and long jobs get isolated?
[07:53] Ethan Park: yes per-route overrides are supported. Redwood console lets you pin routes to a profile. That is a good approach for Auriga since you can tag critical vs non-critical.
[08:03] Devon Li: about token guards: we saw 429s when a large single request came in. Any recommended structure?
[08:10] Ethan Park: recommended: set a hard token-cap to protect slot-level memory, a soft token-cap for admission grading, and a large-request policy that routes to a background lane with looser latency SLO but guaranteed throughput. Users get 429 for interactive lane if they exceed; background processes get queued.
[08:26] Carlos Mendes: background lane meaning separate pool?
[08:28] Ethan Park: correct — separate GPU pool or lower-priority slot set. For onprem you can create a dedicated set for background processing.
[08:36] Lena Ortiz: question on quantization fallback latency: if we route to int4 fallback, do we see jitter or consistent improvement?
[08:43] Ethan Park: usually consistent reduction in latency and memory footprint, but some hardware paths are slower for int4 on certain kernels. We recommend testing on your exact hardware. Also fallback transition cost: mid-request re-routing is expensive. Better to route at admission time.
[08:58] Priya Kapoor: ok. Could you sketch a recommended starting config for our setup?
[09:02] Ethan Park: yes — suggested starter for Auriga onprem-stable:
- admission_window_ms: 12
- max_num_seqs: 4 (global), per-route override 8 for short-critical route
- token_soft_cap: 2048
- token_hard_cap: 4096
- heartbeat_prefetch_interval_s: 60
- kv_ttl_s: 300
- hysteresis_ms: 200
- backoff_multiplier: 1.5
- fallback_policy: route non-critical requests to int4 small-model during sustained load > 75% GPU util

[09:35] Devon Li: that helps. Are those admission_window values milliseconds? 12ms seems low; does that include network delay?
[09:42] Ethan Park: yes it's ms. If your network jitter is high, bump to 20-25ms. We'll validate via the script.
[09:48] Maya Chen: also think about pre-warming KV for known frequent prefixes at deploy time. We can set a deploy hook to prime certain keys.
[09:57] Priya Kapoor: great, we have a small set of canonical prompts to prime.
[10:01] Carlos Mendes: how do we measure the cost tradeoff? prefetch increases memory and some IO, but reduces inference token cost. Any ballpark on cost break-even?
[10:10] Ethan Park: depends on hit rates; generally above 40-50% hit you win on token cost and latency. If hit is below 20% prefetch often wastes cycles. That's why the script will show hit distribution.
[10:22] Devon Li: can we run a stress profile that simulates mixed lengths and peak RPS?
[10:26] Maya Chen: yes — we'll include such a load profile in the benchmark artifacts.
[10:30] Ethan Park: we also recommend enabling detailed tracing for a short window to sample KV access patterns — that'll show top-k prefixes and eviction churn.
[10:39] Priya Kapoor: about per-route fallback, how do we maintain auditability and data residency? We must ensure fallback variants don't leave the VPC.
[10:46] Maya Chen: in Private mode all model variants and fallbacks remain in your VPC; no model is routed outside unless you explicitly enable cross-region fallback. Audit logs include route decisions and which model variant served each request.
[10:57] Lena Ortiz: good. Security question, can prefetch keys contain PHI? We need KMS/HSM controls around that.
[11:03] Ethan Park: we recommend storing hashes of PHI-derived keys in KV and access controls around who can see raw text. For KMS, Redwood integrates with customer KMS on private. We'll send the security doc.
[11:13] Devon Li: thanks. circling back to oscillation — any heuristics for quick detection and automatic stabilization?
[11:20] Ethan Park: yes: monitor rolling 1m and 5m throughput deltas and if delta > threshold, increase hysteresis and force fixed windows for 5 minutes. We can provide that as a rule in the console.
[11:33] Carlos Mendes: can Redwood implement that as an automated rule or do we need to script it?
[11:36] Maya Chen: we can implement via the console automations — we'll propose a rule template.
[11:41] Devon Li: what's the expected timeline to get from baseline to production safe?
[11:45] Maya Chen: conservative plan: week 1 exchange scripts and knobs, week 2 run steady-state and capture traces, week 3 iterate knobs and canary, then ramp. So roughly 3 weeks.
[11:55] Priya Kapoor: sounds reasonable.
[11:57] Ethan Park: quick note on kernel variance with int4 on A100: we saw 5-10% latency variance sometimes. If strict latency SLO then prefer int8 or per-route mixed strategy.
[12:08] Devon Li: so for critical route keep int8, non-critical go int4 fallback.
[12:12] Ethan Park: exactly.
[12:14] Maya Chen: ok let's do a short demo of the console to show where these knobs live — Ethan?
[12:18] Ethan Park: sure jumping screenshare... (screen-sharing, short pause)
[12:30] Ethan Park: here's the scheduling profile page — admission window, max_num_seqs, token caps, storyboards for per-route overrides. You can also attach a prefetch list and heartbeat schedule here.
[12:50] Devon Li: can we attach deploy hooks to prime the KV on CI/CD?
[12:54] Ethan Park: yes deploy-hook integration supported for Private. We'll show the sample hook in the docs.
[13:02] Priya Kapoor: I see a console metric for kv_hit_rate — what's the sampling period?
[13:06] Ethan Park: default 30s but configurable. For fine-grained debugging set to 5s for a short window.
[13:12] Carlos Mendes: one more question: are there recommended guardrails for token ceilings to avoid runaway memory use during adversarial requests?
[13:20] Ethan Park: hard token_cap enforced at admission is primary safeguard. Additionally, you can enable max_output_tokens_per_minute quota per API key to prevent repeated long requests. We'll include recommended values based on your workload.
[13:33] Devon Li: ok. quick break? we've been at it a bit.
[13:36] Maya Chen: sure 2 minutes.
[15:00] [note: timestamps resume at 15:00 in the transcript for the later half of call]
[15:00] Maya Chen: ok back on. Let's walk through the concrete test we want Auriga to run. Ethan?
[15:06] Ethan Park: test: 30 minute steady-state, mixed load: 60% short (<=128 tokens), 30% medium (128-512), 10% long (>512). Target RPS to saturate to ~70% GPU util. Capture kv hit hist, cold-start traces, p50/p95/p99 latencies, token rejection counts. Also run a 5 minute 'burst' where RPS is doubled to validate fallback routing.
[15:31] Devon Li: we'll run that in staging. We'll need the script and a small README.
[15:35] Maya Chen: action: Maya send artifacts, Ethan send script and recommended knob diffs, customer run and share traces.
[15:42] Priya Kapoor: how do we interpret token histograms? any thresholds to watch?
[15:46] Ethan Park: watch for long-tail entries and correlation with KV miss events. If there are many single-request long sequences, consider background lane. If many medium sequences cause requeues then lower max_num_seqs.
[15:58] Carlos Mendes: what's normal CPU offload for prefetch? will that impact our control-plane?
[16:03] Ethan Park: prefetch is lightweight but can spike control-plane I/O during large prefetch sets; limit prefetch concurrency. For onprem we recommend async prefetch with bounded concurrency (default 8 threads).
[16:15] Devon Li: any gotchas with HSM/KMS when prefetching hashed keys?
[16:20] Maya Chen: ensure KMS calls aren't synchronous on the hot path — cache decrypted material when possible and audit access. We'll annotate the security doc.
[16:29] Lena Ortiz: ok I think we have enough to get started. A couple clarifications: 1) will redwood run a joint analysis of traces after we upload? 2) who should we send artifacts to?
[16:39] Maya Chen: yes we'll do joint analysis. Send to me and Ethan; we'll upload notes to the deal in the console.
[16:45] Devon Li: perfect. Anything else?
[16:47] Priya Kapoor: one small ask — could Redwood provide recommended knob values for both p95<250ms and p99<500ms?
[16:56] Ethan Park: yes we'll provide two profiles, one latency-optimized and one throughput-optimized, with tradeoffs documented.
[17:02] Maya Chen: ok last wrap up.

[17:15] Maya Chen: exactly. Thanks all.
